{{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Arida|qid=Q657843}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11370047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Arida, Wakayama|Arida City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Arida, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Arida, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Arida City == There are three Ōji shrines in Arida City.
=== Kabuzaka Ōji === [[File:蕪坂塔下王子.jpg|thumb|Kabuzaka Ōji]] From '''Hitotsubo Ōji''', continue southwest, using guideposts and directional stones to navigate through the Kutsukake area, passing between private houses before ascending the '''Hai Pass''' to the ridgeline. The site of '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' (かぶらざかおうじ, also known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' or '''Kabuzaka Pass Ōji''') is located along a flat path with '''Manyōshū poetry monuments''', overlooking '''Shimozu Bay'''.
While *Chūyūki* (1109, Ten'nin 2, November 6 entry) mentions "Kafurazaka" and *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 11 entry) refers to "Kafura-zaka," it does not explicitly mention a shrine. The first reference to the shrine appears in *Minkeiki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry).<ref>“Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary” Editorial Committee [1985: 288]</ref>
A shrine known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' was built at the site but was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 76]</ref> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* noted that the former site had become a bamboo grove, but by the time Nishi conducted a survey, it had been converted into a farmland road.<ref>Nishi [1987: 111]</ref> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida>{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.arida.lg.jp/kyouiku/culturalproperties.html|title=Cultural Properties of Arida City|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2015-02-08}}</ref>
* '''Location:''' 427 Hata, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子.jpg|thumb|Yamaguchi Ōji]] Descending from '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' toward '''Kii-Miyahara Station''' on the '''Kisei Main Line''', the former shrine site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''' (やまぐちおうじ) is marked by a shrine foundation and guidepost.
*Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) refers to "Kaburazaka Yamaguchi Ōji," while *Raisukeikyōki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) refers to a shrine in '''Miyahara'''. During the Edo period, it was known as '''Yamaguchi Ōji Shrine''' or '''Kabutsuchi Ōji'''.<ref name="Hiroba">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.city.arida.lg.jp/gaiyou/yamaguchiojiatohiroba.html|title=Yamaguchi Ōji Site Plaza|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2012-02-08}}</ref>
The shrine was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 77]</ref> Nishi noted that the area had become a '''fruit sorting facility''', where a '''temizu-bachi''' (water basin) remained.<ref>Nishi [1987: 112]</ref> In 1991 (Heisei 3), local volunteers rebuilt the shrine and set up a '''gazebo''' and '''benches''' for visitors.<ref name="Hiroba"/> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida/>
* '''Location:''' 441-1 Dō, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Itoga Ōji === [[File:糸我王子.jpg|thumb|Itoga Ōji]] South of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', after crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' and '''Route 42''', the '''Itoga Pass''' climb begins, where '''Itoga Ōji''' (いとがおうじ, also known as *Inogaoji*) is located.<ref name="Nishi_1987_113">Nishi [1987: 113]</ref>
It is not mentioned in *Chūyūki* (1109), but *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) states "Next visit to Itoga Ōji, also known as the rugged Itoga Mountain." *Minkeiki* (1109, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) also records the existence of "Itoga Ōji."<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
In the Edo period, '''Itoga Ōji''' was referred to as '''Kami Ōji''', while a separate small shrine called '''Mizu Ōji''' also existed (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*).<ref name="Nishi_1987_113"/> The shrine remained until the early '''Meiji era''', but in 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into '''Inari Shrine'''.<ref>Nishi [1987: 113], Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 930 Nakaban, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
== Surrounding Information ==
=== Itoga Pass and the Kii Route === Between '''Itoga Ōji''' and the next '''Sakagawa Ōji''' (in Yuasa Town) lies the '''Itoga Pass'''. Leaving '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', travelers would cross the '''Arida River''' at '''Miyahara Ferry''', where they would encounter '''Itoga Inari Shrine''', a key {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship site|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} along with Kumano faith. The shrine also houses the '''Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum'''.
As the path ascends, a '''guidepost''' and a monument marking the original site of '''Itoga Ōji''' can be found amid orange groves. The road is paved up to the base of the mountain, but from the '''Seven Turns''' (*Nanamagari*) section, it becomes an earthen path. Rest stops and a '''tea house ruin''' mark the way to the summit of '''Itoga Pass'''. The Arida City side of the pass is designated as a '''National Historic Site''' under the '''Cultural Properties Protection Act'''.
<gallery> File:Kiiji, Miyahara ferry 01.jpg|Miyahara Ferry Site File:糸我稲荷神社.jpg|Itoga Inari Shrine File:くまの古道歴史民俗資料館 02.jpg|Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum File:糸我峠への道 01.jpg|Path to Itoga Pass File:糸我峠.jpg|Itoga Pass </gallery>

{{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Arida|qid=Q657843}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11370047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Arida, Wakayama|Arida City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Arida, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Arida, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Arida City == There are three Ōji shrines in Arida City.
=== Kabuzaka Ōji === [[File:蕪坂塔下王子.jpg|thumb|Kabuzaka Ōji]] From '''Hitotsubo Ōji''', continue southwest, using guideposts and directional stones to navigate through the Kutsukake area, passing between private houses before ascending the '''Hai Pass''' to the ridgeline. The site of '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' (かぶらざかおうじ, also known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' or '''Kabuzaka Pass Ōji''') is located along a flat path with '''Manyōshū poetry monuments''', overlooking '''Shimozu Bay'''.
While *Chūyūki* (1109, Ten'nin 2, November 6 entry) mentions "Kafurazaka" and *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 11 entry) refers to "Kafura-zaka," it does not explicitly mention a shrine. The first reference to the shrine appears in *Minkeiki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry).<ref>“Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary” Editorial Committee [1985: 288]</ref>
A shrine known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' was built at the site but was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 76]</ref> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* noted that the former site had become a bamboo grove, but by the time Nishi conducted a survey, it had been converted into a farmland road.<ref>Nishi [1987: 111]</ref> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida>{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.arida.lg.jp/kyouiku/culturalproperties.html|title=Cultural Properties of Arida City|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2015-02-08}}</ref>
* '''Location:''' 427 Hata, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子.jpg|thumb|Yamaguchi Ōji]] Descending from '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' toward '''Kii-Miyahara Station''' on the '''Kisei Main Line''', the former shrine site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''' (やまぐちおうじ) is marked by a shrine foundation and guidepost.
*Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) refers to "Kaburazaka Yamaguchi Ōji," while *Raisukeikyōki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) refers to a shrine in '''Miyahara'''. During the Edo period, it was known as '''Yamaguchi Ōji Shrine''' or '''Kabutsuchi Ōji'''.<ref name="Hiroba">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.city.arida.lg.jp/gaiyou/yamaguchiojiatohiroba.html|title=Yamaguchi Ōji Site Plaza|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2012-02-08}}</ref>
The shrine was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 77]</ref> Nishi noted that the area had become a '''fruit sorting facility''', where a '''temizu-bachi''' (water basin) remained.<ref>Nishi [1987: 112]</ref> In 1991 (Heisei 3), local volunteers rebuilt the shrine and set up a '''gazebo''' and '''benches''' for visitors.<ref name="Hiroba"/> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida/>
* '''Location:''' 441-1 Dō, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Itoga Ōji === [[File:糸我王子.jpg|thumb|Itoga Ōji]] South of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', after crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' and '''Route 42''', the '''Itoga Pass''' climb begins, where '''Itoga Ōji''' (いとがおうじ, also known as *Inogaoji*) is located.<ref name="Nishi_1987_113">Nishi [1987: 113]</ref>
It is not mentioned in *Chūyūki* (1109), but *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) states "Next visit to Itoga Ōji, also known as the rugged Itoga Mountain." *Minkeiki* (1109, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) also records the existence of "Itoga Ōji."<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
In the Edo period, '''Itoga Ōji''' was referred to as '''Kami Ōji''', while a separate small shrine called '''Mizu Ōji''' also existed (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*).<ref name="Nishi_1987_113"/> The shrine remained until the early '''Meiji era''', but in 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into '''Inari Shrine'''.<ref>Nishi [1987: 113], Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 930 Nakaban, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
== Surrounding Information ==
=== Itoga Pass and the Kii Route === Between '''Itoga Ōji''' and the next '''Sakagawa Ōji''' (in Yuasa Town) lies the '''Itoga Pass'''. Leaving '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', travelers would cross the '''Arida River''' at '''Miyahara Ferry''', where they would encounter '''Itoga Inari Shrine''', a key {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship site|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} along with Kumano faith. The shrine also houses the '''Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum'''.
As the path ascends, a '''guidepost''' and a monument marking the original site of '''Itoga Ōji''' can be found amid orange groves. The road is paved up to the base of the mountain, but from the '''Seven Turns''' (*Nanamagari*) section, it becomes an earthen path. Rest stops and a '''tea house ruin''' mark the way to the summit of '''Itoga Pass'''. The Arida City side of the pass is designated as a '''National Historic Site''' under the '''Cultural Properties Protection Act'''.
<gallery> File:Kiiji, Miyahara ferry 01.jpg|Miyahara Ferry Site File:糸我稲荷神社.jpg|Itoga Inari Shrine File:くまの古道歴史民俗資料館 02.jpg|Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum File:糸我峠への道 01.jpg|Path to Itoga Pass File:糸我峠.jpg|Itoga Pass </gallery>

{{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Arida|qid=Q657843}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11370047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Arida, Wakayama|Arida City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Arida, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Arida, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Arida City == There are three Ōji shrines in Arida City.
=== Kabuzaka Ōji === [[File:蕪坂塔下王子.jpg|thumb|Kabuzaka Ōji]] From '''Hitotsubo Ōji''', continue southwest, using guideposts and directional stones to navigate through the Kutsukake area, passing between private houses before ascending the '''Hai Pass''' to the ridgeline. The site of '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' (かぶらざかおうじ, also known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' or '''Kabuzaka Pass Ōji''') is located along a flat path with '''Manyōshū poetry monuments''', overlooking '''Shimozu Bay'''.
While *Chūyūki* (1109, Ten'nin 2, November 6 entry) mentions "Kafurazaka" and *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 11 entry) refers to "Kafura-zaka," it does not explicitly mention a shrine. The first reference to the shrine appears in *Minkeiki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry).<ref>“Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary” Editorial Committee [1985: 288]</ref>
A shrine known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' was built at the site but was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 76]</ref> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* noted that the former site had become a bamboo grove, but by the time Nishi conducted a survey, it had been converted into a farmland road.<ref>Nishi [1987: 111]</ref> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida>{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.arida.lg.jp/kyouiku/culturalproperties.html|title=Cultural Properties of Arida City|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2015-02-08}}</ref>
* '''Location:''' 427 Hata, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子.jpg|thumb|Yamaguchi Ōji]] Descending from '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' toward '''Kii-Miyahara Station''' on the '''Kisei Main Line''', the former shrine site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''' (やまぐちおうじ) is marked by a shrine foundation and guidepost.
*Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) refers to "Kaburazaka Yamaguchi Ōji," while *Raisukeikyōki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) refers to a shrine in '''Miyahara'''. During the Edo period, it was known as '''Yamaguchi Ōji Shrine''' or '''Kabutsuchi Ōji'''.<ref name="Hiroba">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.city.arida.lg.jp/gaiyou/yamaguchiojiatohiroba.html|title=Yamaguchi Ōji Site Plaza|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2012-02-08}}</ref>
The shrine was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 77]</ref> Nishi noted that the area had become a '''fruit sorting facility''', where a '''temizu-bachi''' (water basin) remained.<ref>Nishi [1987: 112]</ref> In 1991 (Heisei 3), local volunteers rebuilt the shrine and set up a '''gazebo''' and '''benches''' for visitors.<ref name="Hiroba"/> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida/>
* '''Location:''' 441-1 Dō, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Itoga Ōji === [[File:糸我王子.jpg|thumb|Itoga Ōji]] South of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', after crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' and '''Route 42''', the '''Itoga Pass''' climb begins, where '''Itoga Ōji''' (いとがおうじ, also known as *Inogaoji*) is located.<ref name="Nishi_1987_113">Nishi [1987: 113]</ref>
It is not mentioned in *Chūyūki* (1109), but *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) states "Next visit to Itoga Ōji, also known as the rugged Itoga Mountain." *Minkeiki* (1109, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) also records the existence of "Itoga Ōji."<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
In the Edo period, '''Itoga Ōji''' was referred to as '''Kami Ōji''', while a separate small shrine called '''Mizu Ōji''' also existed (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*).<ref name="Nishi_1987_113"/> The shrine remained until the early '''Meiji era''', but in 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into '''Inari Shrine'''.<ref>Nishi [1987: 113], Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 930 Nakaban, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
== Surrounding Information ==
=== Itoga Pass and the Kii Route === Between '''Itoga Ōji''' and the next '''Sakagawa Ōji''' (in Yuasa Town) lies the '''Itoga Pass'''. Leaving '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', travelers would cross the '''Arida River''' at '''Miyahara Ferry''', where they would encounter '''Itoga Inari Shrine''', a key {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship site|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} along with Kumano faith. The shrine also houses the '''Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum'''.
As the path ascends, a '''guidepost''' and a monument marking the original site of '''Itoga Ōji''' can be found amid orange groves. The road is paved up to the base of the mountain, but from the '''Seven Turns''' (*Nanamagari*) section, it becomes an earthen path. Rest stops and a '''tea house ruin''' mark the way to the summit of '''Itoga Pass'''. The Arida City side of the pass is designated as a '''National Historic Site''' under the '''Cultural Properties Protection Act'''.
<gallery> File:Kiiji, Miyahara ferry 01.jpg|Miyahara Ferry Site File:糸我稲荷神社.jpg|Itoga Inari Shrine File:くまの古道歴史民俗資料館 02.jpg|Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum File:糸我峠への道 01.jpg|Path to Itoga Pass File:糸我峠.jpg|Itoga Pass </gallery>

{{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Arida|qid=Q657843}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11370047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Arida, Wakayama|Arida City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Arida, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Arida, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Arida City == There are three Ōji shrines in Arida City.
=== Kabuzaka Ōji === [[File:蕪坂塔下王子.jpg|thumb|Kabuzaka Ōji]] From '''Hitotsubo Ōji''', continue southwest, using guideposts and directional stones to navigate through the Kutsukake area, passing between private houses before ascending the '''Hai Pass''' to the ridgeline. The site of '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' (かぶらざかおうじ, also known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' or '''Kabuzaka Pass Ōji''') is located along a flat path with '''Manyōshū poetry monuments''', overlooking '''Shimozu Bay'''.
While *Chūyūki* (1109, Ten'nin 2, November 6 entry) mentions "Kafurazaka" and *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 11 entry) refers to "Kafura-zaka," it does not explicitly mention a shrine. The first reference to the shrine appears in *Minkeiki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry).<ref>“Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary” Editorial Committee [1985: 288]</ref>
A shrine known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' was built at the site but was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 76]</ref> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* noted that the former site had become a bamboo grove, but by the time Nishi conducted a survey, it had been converted into a farmland road.<ref>Nishi [1987: 111]</ref> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida>{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.arida.lg.jp/kyouiku/culturalproperties.html|title=Cultural Properties of Arida City|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2015-02-08}}</ref>
* '''Location:''' 427 Hata, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子.jpg|thumb|Yamaguchi Ōji]] Descending from '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' toward '''Kii-Miyahara Station''' on the '''Kisei Main Line''', the former shrine site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''' (やまぐちおうじ) is marked by a shrine foundation and guidepost.
*Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) refers to "Kaburazaka Yamaguchi Ōji," while *Raisukeikyōki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) refers to a shrine in '''Miyahara'''. During the Edo period, it was known as '''Yamaguchi Ōji Shrine''' or '''Kabutsuchi Ōji'''.<ref name="Hiroba">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.city.arida.lg.jp/gaiyou/yamaguchiojiatohiroba.html|title=Yamaguchi Ōji Site Plaza|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2012-02-08}}</ref>
The shrine was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 77]</ref> Nishi noted that the area had become a '''fruit sorting facility''', where a '''temizu-bachi''' (water basin) remained.<ref>Nishi [1987: 112]</ref> In 1991 (Heisei 3), local volunteers rebuilt the shrine and set up a '''gazebo''' and '''benches''' for visitors.<ref name="Hiroba"/> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida/>
* '''Location:''' 441-1 Dō, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Itoga Ōji === [[File:糸我王子.jpg|thumb|Itoga Ōji]] South of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', after crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' and '''Route 42''', the '''Itoga Pass''' climb begins, where '''Itoga Ōji''' (いとがおうじ, also known as *Inogaoji*) is located.<ref name="Nishi_1987_113">Nishi [1987: 113]</ref>
It is not mentioned in *Chūyūki* (1109), but *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) states "Next visit to Itoga Ōji, also known as the rugged Itoga Mountain." *Minkeiki* (1109, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) also records the existence of "Itoga Ōji."<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
In the Edo period, '''Itoga Ōji''' was referred to as '''Kami Ōji''', while a separate small shrine called '''Mizu Ōji''' also existed (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*).<ref name="Nishi_1987_113"/> The shrine remained until the early '''Meiji era''', but in 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into '''Inari Shrine'''.<ref>Nishi [1987: 113], Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 930 Nakaban, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
== Surrounding Information ==
=== Itoga Pass and the Kii Route === Between '''Itoga Ōji''' and the next '''Sakagawa Ōji''' (in Yuasa Town) lies the '''Itoga Pass'''. Leaving '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', travelers would cross the '''Arida River''' at '''Miyahara Ferry''', where they would encounter '''Itoga Inari Shrine''', a key {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship site|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} along with Kumano faith. The shrine also houses the '''Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum'''.
As the path ascends, a '''guidepost''' and a monument marking the original site of '''Itoga Ōji''' can be found amid orange groves. The road is paved up to the base of the mountain, but from the '''Seven Turns''' (*Nanamagari*) section, it becomes an earthen path. Rest stops and a '''tea house ruin''' mark the way to the summit of '''Itoga Pass'''. The Arida City side of the pass is designated as a '''National Historic Site''' under the '''Cultural Properties Protection Act'''.
<gallery> File:Kiiji, Miyahara ferry 01.jpg|Miyahara Ferry Site File:糸我稲荷神社.jpg|Itoga Inari Shrine File:くまの古道歴史民俗資料館 02.jpg|Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum File:糸我峠への道 01.jpg|Path to Itoga Pass File:糸我峠.jpg|Itoga Pass </gallery>

{{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Arida|qid=Q657843}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11370047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Arida, Wakayama|Arida City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Arida, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Arida, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Arida City == There are three Ōji shrines in Arida City.
=== Kabuzaka Ōji === [[File:蕪坂塔下王子.jpg|thumb|Kabuzaka Ōji]] From '''Hitotsubo Ōji''', continue southwest, using guideposts and directional stones to navigate through the Kutsukake area, passing between private houses before ascending the '''Hai Pass''' to the ridgeline. The site of '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' (かぶらざかおうじ, also known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' or '''Kabuzaka Pass Ōji''') is located along a flat path with '''Manyōshū poetry monuments''', overlooking '''Shimozu Bay'''.
While *Chūyūki* (1109, Ten'nin 2, November 6 entry) mentions "Kafurazaka" and *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 11 entry) refers to "Kafura-zaka," it does not explicitly mention a shrine. The first reference to the shrine appears in *Minkeiki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry).<ref>“Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary” Editorial Committee [1985: 288]</ref>
A shrine known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' was built at the site but was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 76]</ref> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* noted that the former site had become a bamboo grove, but by the time Nishi conducted a survey, it had been converted into a farmland road.<ref>Nishi [1987: 111]</ref> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida>{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.arida.lg.jp/kyouiku/culturalproperties.html|title=Cultural Properties of Arida City|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2015-02-08}}</ref>
* '''Location:''' 427 Hata, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子.jpg|thumb|Yamaguchi Ōji]] Descending from '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' toward '''Kii-Miyahara Station''' on the '''Kisei Main Line''', the former shrine site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''' (やまぐちおうじ) is marked by a shrine foundation and guidepost.
*Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) refers to "Kaburazaka Yamaguchi Ōji," while *Raisukeikyōki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) refers to a shrine in '''Miyahara'''. During the Edo period, it was known as '''Yamaguchi Ōji Shrine''' or '''Kabutsuchi Ōji'''.<ref name="Hiroba">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.city.arida.lg.jp/gaiyou/yamaguchiojiatohiroba.html|title=Yamaguchi Ōji Site Plaza|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2012-02-08}}</ref>
The shrine was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 77]</ref> Nishi noted that the area had become a '''fruit sorting facility''', where a '''temizu-bachi''' (water basin) remained.<ref>Nishi [1987: 112]</ref> In 1991 (Heisei 3), local volunteers rebuilt the shrine and set up a '''gazebo''' and '''benches''' for visitors.<ref name="Hiroba"/> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida/>
* '''Location:''' 441-1 Dō, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Itoga Ōji === [[File:糸我王子.jpg|thumb|Itoga Ōji]] South of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', after crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' and '''Route 42''', the '''Itoga Pass''' climb begins, where '''Itoga Ōji''' (いとがおうじ, also known as *Inogaoji*) is located.<ref name="Nishi_1987_113">Nishi [1987: 113]</ref>
It is not mentioned in *Chūyūki* (1109), but *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) states "Next visit to Itoga Ōji, also known as the rugged Itoga Mountain." *Minkeiki* (1109, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) also records the existence of "Itoga Ōji."<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
In the Edo period, '''Itoga Ōji''' was referred to as '''Kami Ōji''', while a separate small shrine called '''Mizu Ōji''' also existed (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*).<ref name="Nishi_1987_113"/> The shrine remained until the early '''Meiji era''', but in 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into '''Inari Shrine'''.<ref>Nishi [1987: 113], Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 930 Nakaban, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
== Surrounding Information ==
=== Itoga Pass and the Kii Route === Between '''Itoga Ōji''' and the next '''Sakagawa Ōji''' (in Yuasa Town) lies the '''Itoga Pass'''. Leaving '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', travelers would cross the '''Arida River''' at '''Miyahara Ferry''', where they would encounter '''Itoga Inari Shrine''', a key {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship site|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} along with Kumano faith. The shrine also houses the '''Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum'''.
As the path ascends, a '''guidepost''' and a monument marking the original site of '''Itoga Ōji''' can be found amid orange groves. The road is paved up to the base of the mountain, but from the '''Seven Turns''' (*Nanamagari*) section, it becomes an earthen path. Rest stops and a '''tea house ruin''' mark the way to the summit of '''Itoga Pass'''. The Arida City side of the pass is designated as a '''National Historic Site''' under the '''Cultural Properties Protection Act'''.
<gallery> File:Kiiji, Miyahara ferry 01.jpg|Miyahara Ferry Site File:糸我稲荷神社.jpg|Itoga Inari Shrine File:くまの古道歴史民俗資料館 02.jpg|Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum File:糸我峠への道 01.jpg|Path to Itoga Pass File:糸我峠.jpg|Itoga Pass </gallery>

{{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Arida|qid=Q657843}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11370047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Arida, Wakayama|Arida City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Arida, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Arida, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Arida City == There are three Ōji shrines in Arida City.
=== Kabuzaka Ōji === [[File:蕪坂塔下王子.jpg|thumb|Kabuzaka Ōji]] From '''Hitotsubo Ōji''', continue southwest, using guideposts and directional stones to navigate through the Kutsukake area, passing between private houses before ascending the '''Hai Pass''' to the ridgeline. The site of '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' (かぶらざかおうじ, also known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' or '''Kabuzaka Pass Ōji''') is located along a flat path with '''Manyōshū poetry monuments''', overlooking '''Shimozu Bay'''.
While *Chūyūki* (1109, Ten'nin 2, November 6 entry) mentions "Kafurazaka" and *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 11 entry) refers to "Kafura-zaka," it does not explicitly mention a shrine. The first reference to the shrine appears in *Minkeiki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry).<ref>“Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary” Editorial Committee [1985: 288]</ref>
A shrine known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' was built at the site but was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 76]</ref> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* noted that the former site had become a bamboo grove, but by the time Nishi conducted a survey, it had been converted into a farmland road.<ref>Nishi [1987: 111]</ref> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida>{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.arida.lg.jp/kyouiku/culturalproperties.html|title=Cultural Properties of Arida City|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2015-02-08}}</ref>
* '''Location:''' 427 Hata, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子.jpg|thumb|Yamaguchi Ōji]] Descending from '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' toward '''Kii-Miyahara Station''' on the '''Kisei Main Line''', the former shrine site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''' (やまぐちおうじ) is marked by a shrine foundation and guidepost.
*Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) refers to "Kaburazaka Yamaguchi Ōji," while *Raisukeikyōki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) refers to a shrine in '''Miyahara'''. During the Edo period, it was known as '''Yamaguchi Ōji Shrine''' or '''Kabutsuchi Ōji'''.<ref name="Hiroba">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.city.arida.lg.jp/gaiyou/yamaguchiojiatohiroba.html|title=Yamaguchi Ōji Site Plaza|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2012-02-08}}</ref>
The shrine was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 77]</ref> Nishi noted that the area had become a '''fruit sorting facility''', where a '''temizu-bachi''' (water basin) remained.<ref>Nishi [1987: 112]</ref> In 1991 (Heisei 3), local volunteers rebuilt the shrine and set up a '''gazebo''' and '''benches''' for visitors.<ref name="Hiroba"/> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida/>
* '''Location:''' 441-1 Dō, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Itoga Ōji === [[File:糸我王子.jpg|thumb|Itoga Ōji]] South of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', after crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' and '''Route 42''', the '''Itoga Pass''' climb begins, where '''Itoga Ōji''' (いとがおうじ, also known as *Inogaoji*) is located.<ref name="Nishi_1987_113">Nishi [1987: 113]</ref>
It is not mentioned in *Chūyūki* (1109), but *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) states "Next visit to Itoga Ōji, also known as the rugged Itoga Mountain." *Minkeiki* (1109, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) also records the existence of "Itoga Ōji."<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
In the Edo period, '''Itoga Ōji''' was referred to as '''Kami Ōji''', while a separate small shrine called '''Mizu Ōji''' also existed (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*).<ref name="Nishi_1987_113"/> The shrine remained until the early '''Meiji era''', but in 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into '''Inari Shrine'''.<ref>Nishi [1987: 113], Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 930 Nakaban, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
== Surrounding Information ==
=== Itoga Pass and the Kii Route === Between '''Itoga Ōji''' and the next '''Sakagawa Ōji''' (in Yuasa Town) lies the '''Itoga Pass'''. Leaving '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', travelers would cross the '''Arida River''' at '''Miyahara Ferry''', where they would encounter '''Itoga Inari Shrine''', a key {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship site|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} along with Kumano faith. The shrine also houses the '''Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum'''.
As the path ascends, a '''guidepost''' and a monument marking the original site of '''Itoga Ōji''' can be found amid orange groves. The road is paved up to the base of the mountain, but from the '''Seven Turns''' (*Nanamagari*) section, it becomes an earthen path. Rest stops and a '''tea house ruin''' mark the way to the summit of '''Itoga Pass'''. The Arida City side of the pass is designated as a '''National Historic Site''' under the '''Cultural Properties Protection Act'''.
<gallery> File:Kiiji, Miyahara ferry 01.jpg|Miyahara Ferry Site File:糸我稲荷神社.jpg|Itoga Inari Shrine File:くまの古道歴史民俗資料館 02.jpg|Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum File:糸我峠への道 01.jpg|Path to Itoga Pass File:糸我峠.jpg|Itoga Pass </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (有田市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Arida|qid=Q657843}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11370047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Arida, Wakayama|Arida City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Arida, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Arida, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Arida City == There are three Ōji shrines in Arida City.
=== Kabuzaka Ōji === [[File:蕪坂塔下王子.jpg|thumb|Kabuzaka Ōji]] From '''Hitotsubo Ōji''', continue southwest, using guideposts and directional stones to navigate through the Kutsukake area, passing between private houses before ascending the '''Hai Pass''' to the ridgeline. The site of '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' (かぶらざかおうじ, also known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' or '''Kabuzaka Pass Ōji''') is located along a flat path with '''Manyōshū poetry monuments''', overlooking '''Shimozu Bay'''.
While *Chūyūki* (1109, Ten'nin 2, November 6 entry) mentions "Kafurazaka" and *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 11 entry) refers to "Kafura-zaka," it does not explicitly mention a shrine. The first reference to the shrine appears in *Minkeiki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry).<ref>“Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary” Editorial Committee [1985: 288]</ref>
A shrine known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' was built at the site but was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 76]</ref> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* noted that the former site had become a bamboo grove, but by the time Nishi conducted a survey, it had been converted into a farmland road.<ref>Nishi [1987: 111]</ref> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida>{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.arida.lg.jp/kyouiku/culturalproperties.html|title=Cultural Properties of Arida City|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2015-02-08}}</ref>
* '''Location:''' 427 Hata, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子.jpg|thumb|Yamaguchi Ōji]] Descending from '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' toward '''Kii-Miyahara Station''' on the '''Kisei Main Line''', the former shrine site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''' (やまぐちおうじ) is marked by a shrine foundation and guidepost.
*Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) refers to "Kaburazaka Yamaguchi Ōji," while *Raisukeikyōki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) refers to a shrine in '''Miyahara'''. During the Edo period, it was known as '''Yamaguchi Ōji Shrine''' or '''Kabutsuchi Ōji'''.<ref name="Hiroba">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.city.arida.lg.jp/gaiyou/yamaguchiojiatohiroba.html|title=Yamaguchi Ōji Site Plaza|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2012-02-08}}</ref>
The shrine was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 77]</ref> Nishi noted that the area had become a '''fruit sorting facility''', where a '''temizu-bachi''' (water basin) remained.<ref>Nishi [1987: 112]</ref> In 1991 (Heisei 3), local volunteers rebuilt the shrine and set up a '''gazebo''' and '''benches''' for visitors.<ref name="Hiroba"/> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida/>
* '''Location:''' 441-1 Dō, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Itoga Ōji === [[File:糸我王子.jpg|thumb|Itoga Ōji]] South of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', after crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' and '''Route 42''', the '''Itoga Pass''' climb begins, where '''Itoga Ōji''' (いとがおうじ, also known as *Inogaoji*) is located.<ref name="Nishi_1987_113">Nishi [1987: 113]</ref>
It is not mentioned in *Chūyūki* (1109), but *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) states "Next visit to Itoga Ōji, also known as the rugged Itoga Mountain." *Minkeiki* (1109, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) also records the existence of "Itoga Ōji."<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
In the Edo period, '''Itoga Ōji''' was referred to as '''Kami Ōji''', while a separate small shrine called '''Mizu Ōji''' also existed (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*).<ref name="Nishi_1987_113"/> The shrine remained until the early '''Meiji era''', but in 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into '''Inari Shrine'''.<ref>Nishi [1987: 113], Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 930 Nakaban, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
== Surrounding Information ==
=== Itoga Pass and the Kii Route === Between '''Itoga Ōji''' and the next '''Sakagawa Ōji''' (in Yuasa Town) lies the '''Itoga Pass'''. Leaving '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', travelers would cross the '''Arida River''' at '''Miyahara Ferry''', where they would encounter '''Itoga Inari Shrine''', a key {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship site|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} along with Kumano faith. The shrine also houses the '''Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum'''.
As the path ascends, a '''guidepost''' and a monument marking the original site of '''Itoga Ōji''' can be found amid orange groves. The road is paved up to the base of the mountain, but from the '''Seven Turns''' (*Nanamagari*) section, it becomes an earthen path. Rest stops and a '''tea house ruin''' mark the way to the summit of '''Itoga Pass'''. The Arida City side of the pass is designated as a '''National Historic Site''' under the '''Cultural Properties Protection Act'''.
<gallery> File:Kiiji, Miyahara ferry 01.jpg|Miyahara Ferry Site File:糸我稲荷神社.jpg|Itoga Inari Shrine File:くまの古道歴史民俗資料館 02.jpg|Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum File:糸我峠への道 01.jpg|Path to Itoga Pass File:糸我峠.jpg|Itoga Pass </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (有田市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Arida|WD=Q657843}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11370047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Arida, Wakayama|Arida City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Arida, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Arida, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Arida City == There are three Ōji shrines in Arida City.
=== Kabuzaka Ōji === [[File:蕪坂塔下王子.jpg|thumb|Kabuzaka Ōji]] From '''Hitotsubo Ōji''', continue southwest, using guideposts and directional stones to navigate through the Kutsukake area, passing between private houses before ascending the '''Hai Pass''' to the ridgeline. The site of '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' (かぶらざかおうじ, also known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' or '''Kabuzaka Pass Ōji''') is located along a flat path with '''Manyōshū poetry monuments''', overlooking '''Shimozu Bay'''.
While *Chūyūki* (1109, Ten'nin 2, November 6 entry) mentions "Kafurazaka" and *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 11 entry) refers to "Kafura-zaka," it does not explicitly mention a shrine. The first reference to the shrine appears in *Minkeiki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry).<ref>“Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary” Editorial Committee [1985: 288]</ref>
A shrine known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' was built at the site but was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 76]</ref> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* noted that the former site had become a bamboo grove, but by the time Nishi conducted a survey, it had been converted into a farmland road.<ref>Nishi [1987: 111]</ref> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida>{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.arida.lg.jp/kyouiku/culturalproperties.html|title=Cultural Properties of Arida City|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2015-02-08}}</ref>
* '''Location:''' 427 Hata, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子.jpg|thumb|Yamaguchi Ōji]] Descending from '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' toward '''Kii-Miyahara Station''' on the '''Kisei Main Line''', the former shrine site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''' (やまぐちおうじ) is marked by a shrine foundation and guidepost.
*Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) refers to "Kaburazaka Yamaguchi Ōji," while *Raisukeikyōki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) refers to a shrine in '''Miyahara'''. During the Edo period, it was known as '''Yamaguchi Ōji Shrine''' or '''Kabutsuchi Ōji'''.<ref name="Hiroba">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.city.arida.lg.jp/gaiyou/yamaguchiojiatohiroba.html|title=Yamaguchi Ōji Site Plaza|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2012-02-08}}</ref>
The shrine was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 77]</ref> Nishi noted that the area had become a '''fruit sorting facility''', where a '''temizu-bachi''' (water basin) remained.<ref>Nishi [1987: 112]</ref> In 1991 (Heisei 3), local volunteers rebuilt the shrine and set up a '''gazebo''' and '''benches''' for visitors.<ref name="Hiroba"/> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida/>
* '''Location:''' 441-1 Dō, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Itoga Ōji === [[File:糸我王子.jpg|thumb|Itoga Ōji]] South of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', after crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' and '''Route 42''', the '''Itoga Pass''' climb begins, where '''Itoga Ōji''' (いとがおうじ, also known as *Inogaoji*) is located.<ref name="Nishi_1987_113">Nishi [1987: 113]</ref>
It is not mentioned in *Chūyūki* (1109), but *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) states "Next visit to Itoga Ōji, also known as the rugged Itoga Mountain." *Minkeiki* (1109, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) also records the existence of "Itoga Ōji."<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
In the Edo period, '''Itoga Ōji''' was referred to as '''Kami Ōji''', while a separate small shrine called '''Mizu Ōji''' also existed (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*).<ref name="Nishi_1987_113"/> The shrine remained until the early '''Meiji era''', but in 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into '''Inari Shrine'''.<ref>Nishi [1987: 113], Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 930 Nakaban, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
== Surrounding Information ==
=== Itoga Pass and the Kii Route === Between '''Itoga Ōji''' and the next '''Sakagawa Ōji''' (in Yuasa Town) lies the '''Itoga Pass'''. Leaving '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', travelers would cross the '''Arida River''' at '''Miyahara Ferry''', where they would encounter '''Itoga Inari Shrine''', a key {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship site|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} along with Kumano faith. The shrine also houses the '''Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum'''.
As the path ascends, a '''guidepost''' and a monument marking the original site of '''Itoga Ōji''' can be found amid orange groves. The road is paved up to the base of the mountain, but from the '''Seven Turns''' (*Nanamagari*) section, it becomes an earthen path. Rest stops and a '''tea house ruin''' mark the way to the summit of '''Itoga Pass'''. The Arida City side of the pass is designated as a '''National Historic Site''' under the '''Cultural Properties Protection Act'''.
<gallery> File:Kiiji, Miyahara ferry 01.jpg|Miyahara Ferry Site File:糸我稲荷神社.jpg|Itoga Inari Shrine File:くまの古道歴史民俗資料館 02.jpg|Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum File:糸我峠への道 01.jpg|Path to Itoga Pass File:糸我峠.jpg|Itoga Pass </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (有田市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Arida|WD=Q657843}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Arida, Wakayama|Arida City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Arida, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Arida, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Arida City == There are three Ōji shrines in Arida City.
=== Kabuzaka Ōji === [[File:蕪坂塔下王子.jpg|thumb|Kabuzaka Ōji]] From '''Hitotsubo Ōji''', continue southwest, using guideposts and directional stones to navigate through the Kutsukake area, passing between private houses before ascending the '''Hai Pass''' to the ridgeline. The site of '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' (かぶらざかおうじ, also known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' or '''Kabuzaka Pass Ōji''') is located along a flat path with '''Manyōshū poetry monuments''', overlooking '''Shimozu Bay'''.
While *Chūyūki* (1109, Ten'nin 2, November 6 entry) mentions "Kafurazaka" and *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 11 entry) refers to "Kafura-zaka," it does not explicitly mention a shrine. The first reference to the shrine appears in *Minkeiki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry).<ref>“Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary” Editorial Committee [1985: 288]</ref>
A shrine known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' was built at the site but was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 76]</ref> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* noted that the former site had become a bamboo grove, but by the time Nishi conducted a survey, it had been converted into a farmland road.<ref>Nishi [1987: 111]</ref> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida>{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.arida.lg.jp/kyouiku/culturalproperties.html|title=Cultural Properties of Arida City|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2015-02-08}}</ref>
* '''Location:''' 427 Hata, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子.jpg|thumb|Yamaguchi Ōji]] Descending from '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' toward '''Kii-Miyahara Station''' on the '''Kisei Main Line''', the former shrine site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''' (やまぐちおうじ) is marked by a shrine foundation and guidepost.
*Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) refers to "Kaburazaka Yamaguchi Ōji," while *Raisukeikyōki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) refers to a shrine in '''Miyahara'''. During the Edo period, it was known as '''Yamaguchi Ōji Shrine''' or '''Kabutsuchi Ōji'''.<ref name="Hiroba">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.city.arida.lg.jp/gaiyou/yamaguchiojiatohiroba.html|title=Yamaguchi Ōji Site Plaza|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2012-02-08}}</ref>
The shrine was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 77]</ref> Nishi noted that the area had become a '''fruit sorting facility''', where a '''temizu-bachi''' (water basin) remained.<ref>Nishi [1987: 112]</ref> In 1991 (Heisei 3), local volunteers rebuilt the shrine and set up a '''gazebo''' and '''benches''' for visitors.<ref name="Hiroba"/> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida/>
* '''Location:''' 441-1 Dō, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Itoga Ōji === [[File:糸我王子.jpg|thumb|Itoga Ōji]] South of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', after crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' and '''Route 42''', the '''Itoga Pass''' climb begins, where '''Itoga Ōji''' (いとがおうじ, also known as *Inogaoji*) is located.<ref name="Nishi_1987_113">Nishi [1987: 113]</ref>
It is not mentioned in *Chūyūki* (1109), but *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) states "Next visit to Itoga Ōji, also known as the rugged Itoga Mountain." *Minkeiki* (1109, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) also records the existence of "Itoga Ōji."<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
In the Edo period, '''Itoga Ōji''' was referred to as '''Kami Ōji''', while a separate small shrine called '''Mizu Ōji''' also existed (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*).<ref name="Nishi_1987_113"/> The shrine remained until the early '''Meiji era''', but in 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into '''Inari Shrine'''.<ref>Nishi [1987: 113], Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 930 Nakaban, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
== Surrounding Information ==
=== Itoga Pass and the Kii Route === Between '''Itoga Ōji''' and the next '''Sakagawa Ōji''' (in Yuasa Town) lies the '''Itoga Pass'''. Leaving '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', travelers would cross the '''Arida River''' at '''Miyahara Ferry''', where they would encounter '''Itoga Inari Shrine''', a key {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship site|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} along with Kumano faith. The shrine also houses the '''Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum'''.
As the path ascends, a '''guidepost''' and a monument marking the original site of '''Itoga Ōji''' can be found amid orange groves. The road is paved up to the base of the mountain, but from the '''Seven Turns''' (*Nanamagari*) section, it becomes an earthen path. Rest stops and a '''tea house ruin''' mark the way to the summit of '''Itoga Pass'''. The Arida City side of the pass is designated as a '''National Historic Site''' under the '''Cultural Properties Protection Act'''.
<gallery> File:Kiiji, Miyahara ferry 01.jpg|Miyahara Ferry Site File:糸我稲荷神社.jpg|Itoga Inari Shrine File:くまの古道歴史民俗資料館 02.jpg|Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum File:糸我峠への道 01.jpg|Path to Itoga Pass File:糸我峠.jpg|Itoga Pass </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (有田市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Arida|WD=Q657843}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Arida, Wakayama|Arida City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Arida, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Arida, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Arida City == There are three Ōji shrines in Arida City.
=== Kabuzaka Ōji === [[File:蕪坂塔下王子.jpg|thumb|Kabuzaka Ōji]] From '''Hitotsubo Ōji''', continue southwest, using guideposts and directional stones to navigate through the Kutsukake area, passing between private houses before ascending the '''Hai Pass''' to the ridgeline. The site of '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' (かぶらざかおうじ, also known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' or '''Kabuzaka Pass Ōji''') is located along a flat path with '''Manyōshū poetry monuments''', overlooking '''Shimozu Bay'''.
While *Chūyūki* (1109, Ten'nin 2, November 6 entry) mentions "Kafurazaka" and *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 11 entry) refers to "Kafura-zaka," it does not explicitly mention a shrine. The first reference to the shrine appears in *Minkeiki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry).<ref>“Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary” Editorial Committee [1985: 288]</ref>
A shrine known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' was built at the site but was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 76]</ref> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* noted that the former site had become a bamboo grove, but by the time Nishi conducted a survey, it had been converted into a farmland road.<ref>Nishi [1987: 111]</ref> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida>{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.arida.lg.jp/kyouiku/culturalproperties.html|title=Cultural Properties of Arida City|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2015-02-08}}</ref>
* '''Location:''' 427 Hata, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子.jpg|thumb|Yamaguchi Ōji]] Descending from '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' toward '''Kii-Miyahara Station''' on the '''Kisei Main Line''', the former shrine site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''' (やまぐちおうじ) is marked by a shrine foundation and guidepost.
*Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) refers to "Kaburazaka Yamaguchi Ōji," while *Raisukeikyōki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) refers to a shrine in '''Miyahara'''. During the Edo period, it was known as '''Yamaguchi Ōji Shrine''' or '''Kabutsuchi Ōji'''.<ref name="Hiroba">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.city.arida.lg.jp/gaiyou/yamaguchiojiatohiroba.html|title=Yamaguchi Ōji Site Plaza|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2012-02-08}}</ref>
The shrine was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 77]</ref> Nishi noted that the area had become a '''fruit sorting facility''', where a '''temizu-bachi''' (water basin) remained.<ref>Nishi [1987: 112]</ref> In 1991 (Heisei 3), local volunteers rebuilt the shrine and set up a '''gazebo''' and '''benches''' for visitors.<ref name="Hiroba"/> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida/>
* '''Location:''' 441-1 Dō, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Itoga Ōji === [[File:糸我王子.jpg|thumb|Itoga Ōji]] South of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', after crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' and '''Route 42''', the '''Itoga Pass''' climb begins, where '''Itoga Ōji''' (いとがおうじ, also known as *Inogaoji*) is located.<ref name="Nishi_1987_113">Nishi [1987: 113]</ref>
It is not mentioned in *Chūyūki* (1109), but *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) states "Next visit to Itoga Ōji, also known as the rugged Itoga Mountain." *Minkeiki* (1109, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) also records the existence of "Itoga Ōji."<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
In the Edo period, '''Itoga Ōji''' was referred to as '''Kami Ōji''', while a separate small shrine called '''Mizu Ōji''' also existed (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*).<ref name="Nishi_1987_113"/> The shrine remained until the early '''Meiji era''', but in 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into '''Inari Shrine'''.<ref>Nishi [1987: 113], Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 930 Nakaban, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
== Surrounding Information ==
=== Itoga Pass and the Kii Route === Between '''Itoga Ōji''' and the next '''Sakagawa Ōji''' (in Yuasa Town) lies the '''Itoga Pass'''. Leaving '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', travelers would cross the '''Arida River''' at '''Miyahara Ferry''', where they would encounter '''Itoga Inari Shrine''', a key {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} along with Kumano faith. The shrine also houses the '''Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum'''.
As the path ascends, a '''guidepost''' and a monument marking the original site of '''Itoga Ōji''' can be found amid orange groves. The road is paved up to the base of the mountain, but from the '''Seven Turns''' (*Nanamagari*) section, it becomes an earthen path. Rest stops and a '''tea house ruin''' mark the way to the summit of '''Itoga Pass'''. The Arida City side of the pass is designated as a '''National Historic Site''' under the '''Cultural Properties Protection Act'''.
<gallery> File:Kiiji, Miyahara ferry 01.jpg|Miyahara Ferry Site File:糸我稲荷神社.jpg|Itoga Inari Shrine File:くまの古道歴史民俗資料館 02.jpg|Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum File:糸我峠への道 01.jpg|Path to Itoga Pass File:糸我峠.jpg|Itoga Pass </gallery>

This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Arida, Wakayama|Arida City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Arida, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Arida, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Arida City == There are three Ōji shrines in Arida City.
=== Kabuzaka Ōji === [[File:蕪坂塔下王子.jpg|thumb|Kabuzaka Ōji]] From '''Hitotsubo Ōji''', continue southwest, using guideposts and directional stones to navigate through the Kutsukake area, passing between private houses before ascending the '''Hai Pass''' to the ridgeline. The site of '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' (かぶらざかおうじ, also known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' or '''Kabuzaka Pass Ōji''') is located along a flat path with '''Manyōshū poetry monuments''', overlooking '''Shimozu Bay'''.
While *Chūyūki* (1109, Ten'nin 2, November 6 entry) mentions "Kafurazaka" and *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 11 entry) refers to "Kafura-zaka," it does not explicitly mention a shrine. The first reference to the shrine appears in *Minkeiki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry).<ref>“Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary” Editorial Committee [1985: 288]</ref>
A shrine known as '''Kabuzaka Tōge Ōji''' was built at the site but was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 76]</ref> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* noted that the former site had become a bamboo grove, but by the time Nishi conducted a survey, it had been converted into a farmland road.<ref>Nishi [1987: 111]</ref> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida>{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.arida.lg.jp/kyouiku/culturalproperties.html|title=Cultural Properties of Arida City|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2015-02-08}}</ref>
* '''Location:''' 427 Hata, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子.jpg|thumb|Yamaguchi Ōji]] Descending from '''Kabuzaka Ōji''' toward '''Kii-Miyahara Station''' on the '''Kisei Main Line''', the former shrine site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''' (やまぐちおうじ) is marked by a shrine foundation and guidepost.
*Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) refers to "Kaburazaka Yamaguchi Ōji," while *Raisukeikyōki* (1210, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) refers to a shrine in '''Miyahara'''. During the Edo period, it was known as '''Yamaguchi Ōji Shrine''' or '''Kabutsuchi Ōji'''.<ref name="Hiroba">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.city.arida.lg.jp/gaiyou/yamaguchiojiatohiroba.html|title=Yamaguchi Ōji Site Plaza|publisher=Arida City|accessdate=2012-02-08}}</ref>
The shrine was merged into '''Miyahara Shrine''' in 1908 (Meiji 41).<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 77]</ref> Nishi noted that the area had become a '''fruit sorting facility''', where a '''temizu-bachi''' (water basin) remained.<ref>Nishi [1987: 112]</ref> In 1991 (Heisei 3), local volunteers rebuilt the shrine and set up a '''gazebo''' and '''benches''' for visitors.<ref name="Hiroba"/> The site is designated as a '''municipal cultural property'''.<ref name=Arida/>
* '''Location:''' 441-1 Dō, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Itoga Ōji === [[File:糸我王子.jpg|thumb|Itoga Ōji]] South of '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', after crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' and '''Route 42''', the '''Itoga Pass''' climb begins, where '''Itoga Ōji''' (いとがおうじ, also known as *Inogaoji*) is located.<ref name="Nishi_1987_113">Nishi [1987: 113]</ref>
It is not mentioned in *Chūyūki* (1109), but *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 9 entry) states "Next visit to Itoga Ōji, also known as the rugged Itoga Mountain." *Minkeiki* (1109, Jōgen 4, April 25 entry) also records the existence of "Itoga Ōji."<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
In the Edo period, '''Itoga Ōji''' was referred to as '''Kami Ōji''', while a separate small shrine called '''Mizu Ōji''' also existed (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*).<ref name="Nishi_1987_113"/> The shrine remained until the early '''Meiji era''', but in 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into '''Inari Shrine'''.<ref>Nishi [1987: 113], Heibonsha [1997: 97]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 930 Nakaban, Arida, Wakayama Prefecture
== Surrounding Information ==
=== Itoga Pass and the Kii Route === Between '''Itoga Ōji''' and the next '''Sakagawa Ōji''' (in Yuasa Town) lies the '''Itoga Pass'''. Leaving '''Yamaguchi Ōji''', travelers would cross the '''Arida River''' at '''Miyahara Ferry''', where they would encounter '''Itoga Inari Shrine''', a key {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} along with Kumano faith. The shrine also houses the '''Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum'''.
As the path ascends, a '''guidepost''' and a monument marking the original site of '''Itoga Ōji''' can be found amid orange groves. The road is paved up to the base of the mountain, but from the '''Seven Turns''' (*Nanamagari*) section, it becomes an earthen path. Rest stops and a '''tea house ruin''' mark the way to the summit of '''Itoga Pass'''. The Arida City side of the pass is designated as a '''National Historic Site''' under the '''Cultural Properties Protection Act'''.
<gallery> File:Kiiji, Miyahara ferry 01.jpg|Miyahara Ferry Site File:糸我稲荷神社.jpg|Itoga Inari Shrine File:くまの古道歴史民俗資料館 02.jpg|Kumano Kodo History and Folklore Museum File:糸我峠への道 01.jpg|Path to Itoga Pass File:糸我峠.jpg|Itoga Pass </gallery>
